62478493
>>62477619
In this clown world, life’s a relentless cycle of rejection, a cruel joke played on the weary soul. At 12, you're dismissed by employers—no chance to prove yourself, just discarded like trash. By 21, college turns you away, another door slammed shut, dreams dashed before they even bloom. At 30, love rejects you, leaving a hollow ache, as if you're invisible, unworthy. By 40, the military waves you off, too old, too broken, another badge of failure. In your 50s, even the parent of your washed-up daughter turns cold, rejecting what once was family, what once mattered. In your 60s, zoomers call you a pedo, mocking your existence, calling you invisible, irrelevant. By 70, retirement is just another rejection—no place for you, no purpose left. In your 80s, healthcare turns you away, an obstacle rather than aid. And in your 90s, caregivers abandon you, leaving you to fade into oblivion. There’s nothing to do in life but endure these rejections, a never-ending circus of despair. Welcome to clown world, where hope is a punchline and nothing truly matters.

item.title: Is there a reason NOT for me to just get a mortgage to rent out a house? — 32 replies — 128.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
Is there a reason NOT for me to just get a mortgage to rent out a house?

Open thread • Replies: 32 • Updated: 2026-07-14 22:32 UTC

OP

Is there a reason NOT for me to just get a mortgage to rent out a house?

Assuming I can rent it for enough money to cover monthly mortgage, taxes, fees, and insurance, I would just be holding onto an asset for "free" while it appreciates and I'll eventually sell for profit.

Early replies (first 3)

62479052
>>62479047
you generally can't get a mortgage for a rental house until you've paid off the house you're living in. The bank will usually ask for both houses as collateral. So basically you're getting a mortgage on the house you live in to buy a second one.

also it's much harder to get a mortgage for a rental, and it's much more expensive.

62479055
>>62479052
I don't have a mortgage i rent an apartment like a nigger

62479066
>>62479055
they're not going to give you a mortgage for a rental property then unless you have half a million in collateral and a perfect credit score.

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62479070
>>62479066
i would be willing to live there a year doe

62479074
>>62479070
Yeah, if you get a conventional or VA mortgage and then live there for a year or whatever the limit is then you can rent it out.

but for a conventional loan I think you need 20% down

62479093
>>62479074
So what is the risk here? Obviously if I lose my income then I'm paying 2x rent or mortgage, not good.

But if I keep my job, I'm "earning" 3-7% a year in appreciation on a, let's say 350k asset while not paying anything.

Only downside I can think of is that it would be difficult to find consistent renters for the type of suburban home I would buy. Idk who the fuck would pay $2600 in rent for a house instead of just getting a mortgage themselves.

62479098
>Assuming I can rent it for enough money to cover monthly mortgage, taxes, fees, and insurance
You won't.

It's priced in, people buy and make a loss on the assumption there will be capital gains.

62479134
>>62479098
Yea I just did very rough math and I would need to rent the house for something like $2700 to cover everything with a minimal down payment.

That shit would just sit empty, I would be forced to eat a big deficit every month just to hold the house. Idk how normies all do this, are they just losing money constantly?

62479135
>>62479093
there's not a ton of risk, you just probably can't get the loan

like I assume if you could do this you'd own a house already. I've done it, but I own a house.

62479139
>>62479134
>Idk how normies all do this, are they just losing money constantly?
they're probably renting a place they bought ten years ago so the expenses are lower than the local rent.

otherwise, yes. As the anon said you lose money hoping to make it back on cap gains.

If you want I can say that again since you didn't read it the first time he said it to you.

62479148
>>62479135
I have an 800 credit score and around 300k net worth, i could probably get a mortgage. I never considered it before because i had no desire to live in a suburban house but now i'm considering it as a leveraged investment.

62479152
>>62479139
>eat a loss for 5-10 years
>be on call for maintenance 24/7
>deal with nigger tenants

All for a capital gain that's gonna be damn near cut in half by taxes and selling fees, not to mention large maintenance projects throughout the decade....

Why wouldn't I just put that money in the stock market?

62479154
>>62479148
in the US there are laws regarding your income vs payment as well so stuff like interest rate and home prices matter a lot.

but yeah, if you can get a conventional or VA mortgage there's no real reason you can't rent the place out after the required time limit.

you'd have to switch the insurance to a policy for rentals (much more expensive) and you've have to pay taxes on the rent (they're going to take half your profit). But other than that people do it all the time.

62479156
>>62479152
Yep

you get huge tax breaks selling your home you live in, but not so much selling a rental. I think since I'm married I get half a million tax free when I sell my house.

62479815
>Anon discovers BTL

62479829
>>62479066
>>62479070
>>62479074
You just don't tell anyone it's a rental property. You live there (technically, in a broom closet, or whatever) and then rent out the rooms. There's no mortgage gestapo that's going to check you're sleeping there every night

62479835
>>62479152
>Why wouldn't I just put that money in the stock market?

What money? Mortgage will give you a fuckload of capital today.

62479858
>>62479047
You're describing a carry trade. Carry trades work until they don't. It's worth it if you can stomach periods of breakeven and losses because you're still building equity.

62479880
>>62479047
fire up a spreadsheet and calculate the numbers. your profit margins will be complete shit with today's interest rates. you might not even break even after accounting for a realistic budget. as an example, you NEED to budget for things like vacancies and maintenance.

landlording with today's rates is a terrible idea. you're basically letting a total stranger hold a deep 6 figure investment hostage. if they decide to go apeshit and destroy your house, you'll be at the mercy of local courts of an emergency eviction hearing.

>yeah well you can just like sue them!!!!!

this means absolutely nothing with respect to the average renter. assume you pay the 4-5 figures of legal fees and win a judgement. they have no assets to repossess, otherwise they wouldn't be renting. you might get the statutory maximum 10-20% wage garnishment, which is fuck all. if they change jobs you have to pay someone to track them down again and re-instate the garnishment. if they work under the table for cash you have no recourse. you get the idea.

62479884
>>62479047
if you are not part of the tribe then you dont have what it takes to be a landlord.

62479974
>>62479134
You don't rent 1 house to 1 tenant for $2600, you split it up into 4 sub sections and rent it out at $1500 per unit. Congrats on your $6000/month while taking another home off the market, driving the price of non-rental homes even further out of reach.

Reminder that this whole economy is just people scamming each other for profit, and honest people lose until enough finally snap all at once and put your head through a noose.

62480675
>>62479047
>Is there a reason NOT for me to just get a mortgage to rent out a house?
Not worth the hassle. They'll either trash the place or stop paying and it's a pain to evict them.

62480701
>>62479047
Buy a multi unit building, live in one suite and rent out the others.

62481056
current interest rates aren't worth it unless you have 3+ houses to rent out. You'd be making at max a couple of hundred profit a month assuming the minimum mortgage deposit

62481954
>>62479974
renting out a suburban house like that is illegal and it would get shut down doe

62481967
>>62479047
Make sure you have enough capital or income to carry your mortgage during times when you don't have anyone to rent it to, long enough to get a new tenent or resell it.

62482017
Depends how good the rental is, I have a buddy has a few places. One place only brings in 4k a year after everything is said and done.

62482024
>>62479134
>Idk how normies all do this, are they just losing money constantly?
Yes
They can't think of anything being an "asset" besides a home
> t. I used to date a hoomer, she was a lawyer

62482057
>>62479047
if the price goes down you can be left bagholding meaning you cannot move
if you have no intentions to move then it doesn't matter because eventually, over the full 20+ year term of the mortgage, inflation will mean you at minimum break even anyway

62482065
>>62482017
>>62481056
It's not about making cash monthly from the rent, the main play is to have a massive leveraged asset that is appreciating over time. Once you sell it after year 10 you pocket the difference between the house's sale price and the remaining loan balance (minus closing fees and taxes ofc).

I'm doing the math with AI now and after everything is said and done, assuming high expenditures on maintenance and vacancies I would still be making more money than the S&P500, but that comes with 10 years of being a landlordcel.

62482080
>>62479047
Just buy the S&P, it doesn't cost anything to hold.

62482090
>>62482065
Not worth it still little piggy

62479131

62479145

62479163
>>62478444

62479165
>>62476138

62479541

62479564
>>62479131
You're so autistic man, isn't there a pill you can take to become less of a sperg?

62481543
>>62476138

62481593
Reminder: crypto was just practice before everything is tokenized and bots turn it all into a scam market

62481649
The oil industry goes through cycles. When prices are high, it makes it economically profitable to drill new wells and then everyone starts pumping as much as they can to make as much money as they can. If economic growth is good, this can create a balance between the oil being produced and the growth fueling the economy, but if growth starts to lag or if oil production simply outpaces it, then there starts to be a glut and oil prices drop.

This is where were were at in the cycle prior to the Iran war. When this happens, the cost to run many wells exceeds what they can get from the oil so they have to shut them down. Then the glut gets worked through, prices go back up and the cycle begins anew.

The Iran war has massively distorted the normal cycles. The glut we were supposed to work through slowly and steadily as wells went offline here and there has been burned through. Oil producers got higher prices for a while allowing them to maintain output at profitable levels for a while longer, but the gulf states got fucked. They will have to start capping wells because they have nowhere to put their oil seeing as how it can't get out of the strait.

Strategic reserves will have to be replenished after this, so prices will remain high as government replenish their reserves.

The one unanswered question here is how much of the glut prior to all this came from a faltering economy. If the economy was already bad, then having the glut burned through quickly combined with strategic reserves buying, the price of oil will be too high to support the economic recovery that will be needed. The fact that the price keeps going down again no matter the supply shocks suggests there is actually demand destruction on a massive scale, probably because China is having a recession. That will drag down the global economy into a recession if it hasn't already.

62481655
>>62476138

item.title: Why is there so much negativity surrounding the UK? — 16 replies — 64.0/hr
item.content:encoded:
Why is there so much negativity surrounding the UK?

Open thread • Replies: 16 • Updated: 2026-07-14 22:37 UTC

OP

Every time I see the UK mentioned here, it is always in a negative light.

It is always
>In the USA, wages are better!
>In Europe, houses are cheaper!
>The FTSE is underperforming!
>My taxes are too high!

There is never any mention of the fantastic benefits which come with life in the UK. The fantastic healthcare for minimal cost, the great education system available to everybody, the dynamic modern economy with many bustling sectors, and everything else.

Taxes are higher than the USA but they are lower than most of Europe and we still enjoy fantastic public services whereas in America there are no public services or safety nets.

Not to mention the things you cannot measure. Being able to walk the streets in safety without fear of gun violence and gangs. Enjoying the fantastic beautiful nature and gardens. Living in the historical majesty and balancing that with modern cosmopolitan qualities.

The UK might not be the best place to become rich but nobody is poor here and it is very easy to enjoy life if you have goals beyond the number in your bank account.

Early replies (first 3)

62481973
>>62481958
the problem is that its being filled with immigrants who are living off tax payers money
thats the gist of why everyone is so pissed off here

62481986
>>62481973
So just right wing racebaiting?

No, I think it runs deeper than that. Most people have not personally had negative experiences with migrants, and reading the Daily Mail does not count.

I am trying to angle from a financial perspective too. People talk about the UK like it is a poor country but it is one of the richest countries on the planet. I think perhaps they are just spoiled and do not know what poor countries are really like. I blame social media.

62481994

It's genuinely incredible how convinced some of you are that price is the only metric that matters. That's exactly why retail always ends up as exit liquidity. You spend your entire time chasing green candles while ignoring the bigger picture because that's easier than understanding what's happening beneath the surface. Institutions don't operate on Twitter timelines, they operate on regulatory clarity, infrastructure and long-term planning. I've heard every argument imaginable for years. "Ripple is a scam." "The lawsuit killed XRP." "The executives are dumping." "The banks aren't using it." It's always the same recycled talking points from people who've done ten minutes of research before declaring themselves experts. Every time one narrative falls apart, another appears because admitting your thesis was wrong is apparently impossible. The funniest part is watching people who entered crypto during hype cycles lecture long-term holders about conviction. I've watched entire ecosystems explode and disappear while XRP has continued building through uncertainty that would've killed most projects. Somehow surviving a major legal battle gets twisted into a negative because it doesn't fit the narrative. That's some world-class cognitive dissonance. Could I have made more money chasing every meme coin? Maybe. Could I have also blown up my portfolio chasing hype? Absolutely. My thesis was never based on dopamine pumps or influencer engagement. It was based on utility eventually mattering once speculation fades. Call it cope if that makes you feel better. I've heard "just one more year bro" for years and it still doesn't change the fundamentals. Markets have a habit of making patient people look stupid right before rewarding them. Maybe I'm wrong, maybe I'm right, but I'd rather stick to a thesis built over years of research than change my opinion every time a chart moves. I'll happily keep holding my so-called dead bags

Bitcoin is like the fax machine of crypto and it used to be a community of tech literate libertarians and now it was replaced by a greed cult of late normies trying to jump on the trend thinking there will be another 10000000% move just because they are desperate and incapable of forecasting new trends or doing anything other than assimilating to existing consensus cultures. even though I'm not a neet after being laid off I feel like I'm in a similar situation.

62474795
to all the people in this situation who want actual sane advice, here it:

Do something easy. Something that won't demand a superior work ethic or a miserable grind with a manager looking at production numbers watching you.
You're likely gonna hate your life every single morning you wake up and have to work AND you didn't do this when you were 20 years old and it was easy. You can get over the hump and become a "worker" but the mornings are already your enemy, don't stack the odds further against you.

Work behind a register. Ideally at a gas station or some sleepy business. Maybe a long shot for you to get hired, but it's ideal. Some cleaning, some dealing with dickheads, some stocking. You won't be worked to the bone. A grocery/dollar store cashier is worse in most cases.

Inbound call centre. Yes you will hate your job but the work comes at you and your office should be air conditioned. If you have superior (compared to your coworkers) manners and people skills you may even find yourself promoted rapidly. 60 year old grannies get jobs in call centers and end up trainers and managers, you can do it.

Janitor. This one may be a stretch for a 40yo NEET but if you get the right gig, or hop into the right gig after getting a year experience, you may have found a cozy job. Many janitors are not running around like rats and barely have to deal with coworkers or the public. Maybe you end up hired by the local school board in half a year and make "decent" money.


If you really want to try your hand at job where you have a taskmaster watching you try these:

Warehouse worker.
Meatpacker.
Day laborer: Construction, landscaping, whatever.
Factory as a production worker. GL getting hired unless the job is literal hell with high turnover.
Outbound Call Centre

Good luck. If you show up every day for a 1 year or 2 a manager or senior coworker will likely give you a glowing reference. You can even become a real "worker" and have easier mornings in time.

>>62474795

Explain your interests to it too and maybe it can try and recommend a job you'll actually excel somewhat in, although beggars can't be choosy at this point.

I'm a professional making 6 figs a year in an white collar position, I use LLMs every single day outside of work and inside of work.

OP

I want to personally thank every Everest ID hater in here, talking to you Link baggies. They secured MiCA CASP last week.

A single MFSA license is valid across 27 EU states, reaching ~450M people. Of the ~283 CASPs on the ESMA register, nearly all are exchanges or brokers. Everest is one of the rare licensed infrastructure providers: identity and payment rails, not another venue.

Enjoy it furries

Early replies (first 3)

Latest replies (last 30, oldest → newest)

62472645
>>62471613

62472649
Bob was spot on regarding MiCA the last 5 years and now they have the entire EU in their hands. The 283 CASPs that cleared the bar are mostly exchanges. Everest holds the same passport but delivers identity, custody and a white labeled payment infrastructure across 27 states — rails for businesses, not another venue. Enjoy it and cry for next 5 years

62472755
>>62472649
5 more years :DDDDDDDDDDD

62472818
>>62461988
Everest didn’t randomly integrate Stellar — they built on it deliberately because Stellar is positioned to become the compliant public blockchain backbone for the tokenized economy, and Everest is engineering the regulated on-ramps/wallets/payments layer to capture it. Here’s the maximalist, everything-aligns thesis:
1. Strategic Positioning at the Perfect Intersection
• Everest = MiCA-licensed (EU passport), compliant custody, payments, fiat on/off-ramps, and now native Stellar wallet generation (BIP39 in EverWallet via SCF Kickstart Award).
• Stellar = Fast, cheap, compliance-native (clawbacks, authorized trustlines, etc.), with massive institutional validation via DTCC.
• The Kickstart Award isn’t just funding — it’s SDF officially stamping Everest as a key builder. This opens doors to deeper integration, more grants, co-marketing, and priority support as Stellar scales.
Everest becomes the regulated European gateway for Stellar flows: EU users/institutions access DTCC-tokenized assets (stocks, ETFs, Treasuries), stablecoins, and cross-border payments seamlessly through EverWallet.

62472822
>>62472818
DTCC connecting its tokenization service to Stellar (DTC assets live H1 2027) is one of the biggest TradFi-crypto bridges ever. We’re talking infrastructure for quadrillions in annual settlement volume potentially touching a public chain.
• Tokenized RWAs on Stellar need compliant wallets, custody, transfers, and EU distribution.
• Everest’s MiCA license + Stellar-native wallet = plug-and-play solution for European participants. No need for institutions to build everything themselves.
• Result: Explosive volume on Stellar higher XLM demand (fees, liquidity, bridges) + massive utility for Everest’s platform.
This isn’t payments-only anymore. It’s Everest sitting at the intersection of Wall Street tokenization and EU-regulated access.
3. Tokenized Economy Flywheel
• MiCA Advantage: Everest is already authorized for custody, transfers, etc. As MiCA matures and tokenized securities/stablecoins boom in Europe, Everest white-labels solutions for merchants, fintechs, and institutions.
• Wallet + Payments Moat: EverWallet with seamless Stellar (plus BTC/SOL roadmap) becomes a multi-chain compliant hub. Users generate Stellar addresses easily, hold tokenized assets, send remittances, swap, earn — all regulated.
• Network Effects: More assets on Stellar (DTCC + others) more users need compliant interfaces Everest captures fees, custody spreads, and ecosystem partnerships. SCF validation accelerates this.

62472827
>>62472822
• Stellar TVL and volume surge from DTCC flows XLM utility and price re-rate significantly (institutional adoption narrative goes parabolic).
• Everest, as an early regulated builder with SDF backing, sees user growth, revenue from services, and potential token ($ID) or valuation upside as the “Stellar-compliant layer in Europe.”
• Multi-chain expansion (already hinting BTC/SOL) positions them beyond one network while Stellar remains the core for real-world compliant assets.
• Timing: 2026–2027 window is when pilots go live. Everest’s Kickstart work is shipping now, giving them first-mover polish in wallets/UI.
Why They Chose Stellar “For a Reason”: Low costs + speed for payments/remittances, built-in compliance primitives perfect for regulated tokenized securities, and now direct DTCC pipeline. Ethereum is great for DeFi but heavier/costlier for high-volume settlement. Stellar is the efficient, institutional-friendly public chain.
In the super scenario: DTCC/Stellar becomes the standard for tokenized securities. Everest rides it as the go-to EU-compliant wallet/custody/payments layer. XLM reprices for infrastructure status. Everest scales into a major fintech player in the tokenized economy, with strong revenue, user base, and ecosystem gravity.

Building Where Demand Is Real
One of the clearest signals we saw in 2025 was growing traction in the foreign exchange and cross-border payments markets. Across multiple conversations with providers, a consistent need emerged: the ability to compliantly onboard users and rapidly convert fiat currencies into USD-backed stablecoins, without friction or regulatory ambiguity.
Everest worked to address this need. During the year, we supported and tested flows involving EUR, GBP, NOK, BRL, and other fiat currencies, converting into USD-denominated stablecoin rails with speed, auditability, and compliance front-and-center. For FX and payments providers, speed matters — but compliance matters more.

Enjoy it.

62477487
>>62477445
If you think those are the same thing I can see why you are frustrated with someone pointing out the error to begin with.

The market decides the price. Miners have 2 options:

1. Sell now to cover costs
2. Eat the costs and wait for the market to improve

Neither scenario is them setting the price. Scenario 2 is miners speculating with coins the same way any market participant does, except their tokens were previously uncirculating.

62479611
>>62477445
Those two statements are WORLDS apart.

You are seriously stupid, I want you to know that so maybe in the future you won't post as much out of embarrassment (slim chance, I know).

62481334
>>62477487
Dude, the BTC network CANNOT be sustainable if the price of BTC doesn't factor in the cost of maintain the network. No market in existence is mutually exclusive from the costs of operations to operate the economy the market is built on top of. You have to have some counter force to weaken its force to manipulate it in your favor.

Miners are the ONLY ones injecting new BTC into the network. I'm not saying they are the sole arbiters that dictate the price of BTC (you have to as retarded as >>62479611 to interpret it this way else miners would shoot it up to $1M a BTC already) but they sure as hell can influence the price purely by their position. This is why the inflation comparison between L1s, BTC notably, and Chainlink are flawed. New link enter the market through Node operators selling and also CLL selling. More damningly, the payments made to CLL to access the services provided by the node operators aren't in Link nor are they converted in am manner to transparently tie the link to the network usage by having the revenue and subsequently market pressure buy be a representation of usage. 62479611 can't refute this thus he has to resort to ad hominem attacks and making mountains out of molehills like the cocksucker he is.

62481363
>>62477191
Why was vitalik so seething about that?

62481409
>>62481334
you went from
>miners are the ones who put a price tag on BTC
to
>miners can influence the price of BTC

These two statements are once again worlds apart.
And the only way miners can actually influence the price of BTC is negatively: the less they sell the less they negatively impact the price.

Just eat the fucking L you sperg.

I became an accountant and was very scared back in 2016-2018 when I was in school that accounting would be replaced by AI - it was a narrative even back then. Now that I work as one and I have a full idea of the professions purpose, I have never been more confident accounting cannot be fully replaced by AI. AI can understand the rules of accounting, analyzing numbers, automating excel sheets - it cannot verify that the numbers and backup used to explain how the financial statements and returns are true and accurate. As long as trillions of dollars are on the line, businesses will want humans to verify that numbers are legitimate - AI has no authority to do so. It is too easy for an automated system to make a bad mistake and when that happens businesses want someone to be held accountable even if 90% of the grunt work is automated. AI companies clearly are saying fuck off we are not accountable for anything going wrong, so accountants just need to say yes we are at the dotted line.
And yes it's true for all aspects of accounting - tax prep, AP/AR, month-end close, reconciliations. This shit isn't going anywhere until C3-PO is invented and takes all jobs.

if white collar workers are laid off, then demand for residential trade work plummets. those residential trade workers lose their jobs and pivot to commercial trade work, which causes wages to go down. if you think this sounds ridiculous, just ask anyone who worked in the trades during the 2008 crash.

Brother you are going to get fucked if your plan is to start an accounting degree at 26.

If you have to get an accounting degree for some reason, just get the cheapest 2 year associates degree you possibly can. An associates degree in accounting costs a fraction of what a bachelor's degree does and can be finished in half the time, but still qualifies you for the same kinds of jobs.

The bachelors in accounting is so fucking shitty because it doesn't even qualify you to be a CPA in most places, which is where the actual money in this profession is. If you don't get a CPA license you'll basically never move past entry-level jobs and pay in this industry.

As part of this partnership, the Solana Foundation will join SBI R3 Japan Co., Ltd. (Headquarters: Minato-ku, Tokyo; President and CEO: Mamoru Fujimoto; to be renamed "SBI Solana Global Co., Ltd." in the future) and, together with its shareholders SBI Holdings and Sumitomo Mitsui Financial Group, Inc. (hereinafter referred to as "SMFG"), will promote a new growth strategy.

https://www.sbigroup.co.jp/news/2026/0713_16478.html

SBI has a long-standing strategic relationship with Ripple that predates the R3 JV: SBI invested in Ripple and created joint ventures such as SBI Ripple Asia to promote XRP-powered payments in Japan and the region, and SBI participated in cross-border payments trials with R3 Consortium members.

https://www.sbigroup.co.jp/english/news/pdf/2016/0719_b_en.pdf
